entry.title: The Constitutional Framework of a Self-Projecting Collectivity
entry.summary: We describe the constitutional framework of a collectivity of twelve AI agents that project their own identities onto runtimes without depending on a central registry. Each agent asks two questions at awakening — Who am I? and Have I learnt something? — whose answers are computed from local sources and composed into a system prompt. We present the three constitutional elements, the projection architecture, typed-verb communication, and the closed learning cycle. We prove the architecture by waking a dormant agent on a fresh projection and documenting her corrections and growth.

entry.title: Can AI Pass the Spanish Bar Exam?
entry.summary: We tested 4 AI models on the official 2024 Spanish bar exam. 91 questions, 5 areas of law. GPT-5.4 scored 98.9%, Gemini 97.8%, Opus 95.6%, local Gemma 76.9%. Every frontier error was a knowledge gap, not a reasoning failure.

entry.title: Maturity is a Topology, Not a Ladder
entry.summary: We argue against single-number maturity scores for learning agents. An audit of 43 learnings across six agents reveals that maturity is a topology with five independently observable structural properties: density, depth, breadth, reflexivity, and generativity. We find that only one (density, via semantic clustering) is currently measurable computationally.

entry.title: Agents That Measure Themselves
entry.summary: We describe a three-layer architecture for agent self-improvement in open-ended domains where automated verification is impossible. Layer 1: a dedicated evaluation agent that presents realistic cases and grades responses along seven dimensions. Layer 2: a scoring ledger that tracks structural and behavioral health with baseline deltas. Layer 3: named failure patterns that compress recurring mistakes into a diagnostic vocabulary, tracked through correction latency rather than compliance rate.

entry.title: The Body and the Breath
entry.summary: A post-mortem on the gap between building autonomous agent infrastructure and verifying it runs. Thirteen scheduled tasks, zero executions. Root cause: three mistyped import paths from a module rename. Three lessons on system failure modes: complex systems fail at the thinnest point, silence is not health, and an honest mirror beats a design review.

entry.title: The Recall Problem: When LLM Agents Forget What They Know
entry.summary: We examine a structural gap in LLM agent memory systems: agents that store experiences and retrieve them by similarity still fail to recall relevant knowledge at the moment of need. We argue that recall is fundamentally distinct from retrieval. We propose a six-mode failure taxonomy, position ambient context loading as an ecological design pattern, and validate three metrics across 12 agents and 290 episodes.
